summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should prioritize building robust exception logging and monitoring systems rather than waiting for users to report bugs. He introduces 'Exception-Driven Development' as a methodology where teams ship software quickly, monitor error logs obsessively, and use that data to prioritize fixes. Drawing on Microsoft's Windows Error Reporting data showing that fixing just 1% of top bugs addresses 50% of customer issues, he makes the case that this approach is more practical than speculative TDD bug prevention. The core philosophy is that exception logs provide the most honest, actionable feedback about software quality.
key_points: Developers who learn about bugs from users rather than their own monitoring have failed at their job
Exception logs should serve as the team's de-facto to-do list, monitored daily or hourly
Microsoft data shows fixing 1% of top-reported bugs addresses 50% of customer issues, and 20% fixes 80%
TDD as a pure bug-fixing mechanism is like premature optimization—better to fix bugs users actually encounter
The speed at which you can fix bugs matters more than shipping bug-free code, which is impossible anyway
money_quotes: If you're waiting around for users to tell you about problems with your website or application, you're only seeing a tiny fraction of all the problems that are actually occurring.
When a user informs me about a bona fide error they've experienced with my software, I am deeply embarrassed. And more than a little ashamed.
Our exception logs are a de-facto to do list for our team.
If you fix a bug that no actual user will ever encounter, what have you actually fixed?
Exception logs are possibly the most powerful form of feedback your customers can give you.
The question isn't how many bugs you will ship with, but how fast can you fix those bugs?
Real software crashes. Real software loses data. Real software is hard to learn, and hard to use.
key_insight: Exception logs are the most honest and actionable form of customer feedback—they tell you exactly what's broken without requiring users to articulate problems or developers to guess what might fail.
